author: @screen-protector
text:
Just a few wee notes from myself. It's better to use the series to get more volts as this will be less amps = less loss on the wiring and thiner wires = cheaper to build the whole system.

Just keep in mind you need a MPPT controller, the one I'm building now to stabilize the charging power to the batteries and load. Without it, with a regular controller, you won't have:

1. Charging phases which are 3 and some for a quick charge and other to sustain. Without it you're killing the battery quicker.
2. If no controller, no protection, can damage batteries and other equipment.
3. The charging itself if no MPPT controller = higher losses, but synchronous controller like MPPT max out the possible charge.

The one I'm building now goes up to 80V on the input and max 30A, but firmware is limited to 1kW. It's a great project and open source with an app for your smartphone. I'll make a video about the build soon on this/my Youtube channel.

author: @zeshan144
text: Ive built myself a similar generator. I would like to work out the power output of it but not sure how to do it. I understand I need to put the generator under load and that volts x amps = power. I can measure volts easily but struggle to measure amps. Can I do it without a precision calibrated load like you have? I have a couple of multimeters to measure volts and amps at the same time. What sort of set up can I do with multimeters and LEDS or resistors as a load? I have built a rectifier for it too

author: @emilalmberg1096
text:
You are making several mistakes!
You measure the efficiency of the entire system, generator, rectifier, thin cables and a non-linear LED!
The worst is that you connect 3 voltages that are out of phase with each other.
Start over, connect only one resistor per phase, measure voltage and current, multiply for power...

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Not knowing what you want to do and remain flexable, It may  be nice to simply terminate  three coils with 6 terminals. That gives you the flexibility to  try different ways to recitify three phases.  

If you seperately rectify the output of each of three phases, you may as well as also add three capacitors, one across the output of each bridge rectifyer.  This way, unloaded, you'll get closer to 3× the peek voltage of each coil, rather than the sum of three different phases. The down side of three sets of bridge rectifyers with output in serries is a voltage drop in the amount of six diodes.

author: @sailaway8244
text: Why push the volts? anything above 55v outside you start seriously breaching IEE regulations (RCDs etc) surely you're safer limiting the volts at 13 m/s (likely top power  output) ? Va x Ia = power .... there's a reason why site transformers are limited to 110v (55v centre tapped)🤔48v peak output maybe the best option but 24v or 12v shouldn't be discounted depending on location/system

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: stick a transformer in there mate

replies.author: @sailaway8244
replies.text: @IC86688 tru , but is it worth the risk ? 55v is the limit for a reason... the cost between a cable size or 2 is negligible unless you're talking long distances (volt drop)

replies.author: @sailaway8244
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering why suffer the losses involved in gearing up to then transform the voltage down to a safe limit and over complicate the build?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @sailaway8244 there is a limit to how fast you can turn something - you can't make your arm turn faster it is just physically impossible - but you can use gears to do it until you reach a torque you can't shift - that way you are getting the most efficient output from your machine. You can just turn it slower in which case the volts will drop but you are using your machine in a very very inefficient way - almost making it not worth doing. But then everything is safe if you just don't do it.

replies.author: @sailaway8244
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering agreed, just highlighting the issues with breaking the 55v extra low voltage barrier that you'd break gearing up with 2 or 3 sets of gears.... maybe a form of speed switch maybe a future project switching coil configurations with wind speed something like a star-delta starter would do using trigger voltages on a relay or two.

text: I am having difficulty wrapping the coils. I have two on now and although they each read about 60 oh one seems to be producing more than the other .  I  The weaker coil is the outermost one and is farther from the magnets. Not sure how I am going to stuff a third coil on. The ends are square allowing 4 long threaded rods to hold the top and bottom together. author: @William_Hada
text: I finished building one of these myself last week from your older STL file. ( I designed a longer end cap ) and was very happy with how it turned out. I used 3 coils with 200 turns each of 0.2 mm O.D. wire, about 90 ohms each. Wired it in parallel with a full wave rectifier on each coil. I spun it with a drill and it dimly lit a 12v incandescent auto bulb load and produced about 8 volts at 400 milliamps. So it was putting out about 3.2 watts. :)

replies.author: @mikejones-vd3fg
replies.text: Same here although i wish he'd use parametric desing(the downside of using tinkercad i guess) so the files were easier to modify to fit our custom needs. I want to make a smaller version of the fold up turbine and simply scalling doesnt do it,  but thats not easy to do as im finding with my own designs so i dont blame them. Maybe they'll find some benefit in it for themselves as well and start add some to their designs.  I see the'yre reusing the same thigns over and over and if im not mistaken you have to make them from scratch in tinkercad which must not be a problem if youre good at it and it seems like they are, so whatever works.  But if anything needed parametric desing, all these shapes and sizes of gears would be it.

replies.author: @jima3252
replies.text: Do you know what the impedance of one coil is?

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: @mikejones-vd3fg The best solution is to be able to use a CAD program well enough to design your own models from scratch. It is a little painful at first to get to that level but if you  stick with it you will definitely get to that level. There is so much help available from the internet and YouTube to help you get there. Just Google any single problem you are having and you will find the answer to that hurdle. Step by step you will learn it.

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: @jima3252 No, didn't measure AC reluctance. DC resistance is  about 90 ohms.
